title: Guideline on Personal Data Protection Notices
summary: This Guideline provides comprehensive guidance on the Notice and Choice Principle under section 7 of the Personal Data Protection Act 2010. Data users are required to provide data subjects with a written data protection notice containing prescribed information before or at the time of collecting personal data. The notice must be in Bahasa Malaysia and English and must be written in plain language. The Guideline sets out the mandatory content of the notice, permissible formats (including layered and digital notices), the timing and delivery of notices, and special provisions for notices to minors and vulnerable persons.

provisions.title: Purpose and Nature of the Data Protection Notice
provisions.content: The data protection notice (also referred to as a 'privacy notice' or 'personal data notice') is the primary instrument through which a data user fulfils the Notice and Choice Principle under section 7 of the Act. The notice serves to: (a) inform the data subject of the data user's identity and contact details; (b) notify the data subject of the purposes for which their personal data is being collected and will be used; (c) describe the types of personal data being collected; (d) disclose any third parties to whom the data may be disclosed; (e) inform the data subject of their rights; and (f) provide a mechanism for the data subject to exercise their right of choice. A notice that is incomplete, misleading, or incomprehensible does not discharge the data user's obligation under s7.
requirements: The notice must be provided in writing — verbal-only notices do not satisfy the obligation
The notice must be accessible — it must not be buried in fine print or require unreasonable effort to locate
The notice is a living document — it must be updated when processing purposes or key information change
Data subjects must be notified of material changes to the notice

provisions.title: Mandatory Content of the Data Protection Notice
provisions.content: The data protection notice must contain the following mandatory elements as prescribed in the Personal Data Protection Regulations 2013: (1) Description of personal data — the types or categories of personal data being collected (e.g., name, identity card number, contact details, financial information, health information); (2) Purposes of processing — a clear, specific description of all purposes for which the personal data will be used, including any secondary or future uses; (3) Sources of personal data — where personal data is not collected directly from the data subject, the source must be identified; (4) Data subject rights — notification of the right to access, right to correct, right to opt out from direct marketing, and any other applicable rights; (5) Disclosures to third parties — the categories of third parties to whom the data may be disclosed, including regulators, credit bureaus, service providers, and related companies; (6) Cross-border transfers — whether the data will be transferred outside Malaysia and to which categories of countries or organizations; (7) Obligatory or voluntary provision — whether providing the personal data is mandatory or voluntary and the consequences of non-provision; and (8) Data user's identity and contact details — the full legal name, address, and contact information of the data user.
requirements: All 8 mandatory elements must be present — omission of any element is a breach of s7
Purposes must be specific and meaningful — 'improving our services' alone is not sufficient
Third party disclosures must be described by category — not just 'service providers' without further explanation
The notice must identify a specific contact person or channel for data protection queries

provisions.title: Language Requirements
provisions.content: The data protection notice must be provided in both Bahasa Malaysia and English. Where the data user primarily serves a community that uses another language (e.g., Mandarin, Tamil), the notice should also be provided in that language as a matter of good practice, but the Bahasa Malaysia and English versions are the legally operative ones. The translation must be accurate and not misleading. Machine translations without human review are not acceptable. In the event of any inconsistency between the Bahasa Malaysia and English versions, the Bahasa Malaysia version prevails unless the data user expressly states otherwise in the notice itself.
requirements: Both language versions must contain the same information
Translations must be accurate and professionally prepared
Both versions must be displayed with equal prominence
For digital notices, both versions must be available and easily switchable

provisions.title: Timing of the Notice
provisions.content: The data protection notice must be provided to the data subject: (a) at or before the time when the data user first collects the data subject's personal data; (b) where the personal data is collected from a third party (and the data subject was not involved in the collection), as soon as reasonably practicable after the data user first uses or discloses the data, and in any case within 7 days of first use or disclosure; and (c) when there is a material change to the notice that affects existing processing of the data subject's personal data — notification must be given before the new processing begins. The notice must be actively drawn to the data subject's attention — it cannot be merely posted on a website without any indication being given to the data subject that they should read it.
requirements: The notice must be proactively drawn to the data subject's attention, not merely made available
For electronic collections, a link to the notice at the point of data entry is the minimum — a highlighted notice is recommended
For paper-based collections, the notice must accompany or form part of the form
Material change notifications must identify what has changed and why

provisions.title: Layered Notice Approach
provisions.content: Given the volume of information that may need to be communicated in a comprehensive notice, a layered notice approach is recommended and accepted by the Commissioner as satisfying the s7 obligation provided: (a) the first layer (short notice) contains the most important information — identity of data user, main purposes, types of data, and how to access the full notice; (b) the full notice (second layer) contains all mandatory elements and is easily accessible from the first layer (e.g., via a link, QR code, or reference to a specific location); (c) the full notice is genuinely accessible — not hidden behind multiple clicks or paywalls; and (d) any information relevant to the data subject's specific interaction is prominently featured in the first layer, even if a general full notice is provided. A notice that provides general information in the first layer and material transaction-specific information only in an inaccessible second layer does not satisfy the obligation.
requirements: First layer must be immediately visible at the point of data collection
Full notice must be accessible within one click from the first layer in digital channels
The short notice must not be misleading or create a false impression of the full processing activities
Layered notices must be designed with accessibility in mind (screen readers, contrast ratios, font sizes)

provisions.title: Notice for Special Categories of Data Subjects
provisions.content: Special provisions apply where personal data is collected from: (a) Minors (persons under 18 years) — where a data user knows or should know that personal data is being collected from a minor, the notice must be in language and format appropriate for the minor's age and comprehension, and parental or guardian consent must be obtained for minors below 13 years; (b) Persons with disabilities — notices must be provided in accessible formats (e.g., large print, Braille, audio, Easy Read) where the data user knows or should know that a data subject with a disability will be accessing the notice; and (c) Vulnerable persons — where personal data is collected from persons in vulnerable circumstances (e.g., patients in health emergencies), the notice may be deferred until the person is in a position to understand it, but must be provided as soon as reasonably practicable.
requirements: Age verification measures are recommended for online services directed at children
Notices for children must avoid commercial language, images, and psychological techniques
WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility standards are recommended for digital privacy notices
Health sector data users must have clear protocols for providing notices in emergency situations

provisions.title: Notice in the Context of Direct Marketing
provisions.content: Where a data user intends to use personal data for direct marketing purposes, the notice must: (a) expressly state that the personal data will be used for direct marketing; (b) describe the types of marketing communications the data subject may receive; (c) identify any third parties to whom the data may be disclosed for direct marketing purposes; and (d) prominently describe the data subject's right to opt out of direct marketing under section 43 of the Act and provide a simple and free mechanism to exercise that right. The opt-out mechanism must be as accessible as the opt-in. Pre-ticked boxes consenting to direct marketing are not valid. Separate, granular consent for different marketing channels (email, SMS, phone) is recommended.
requirements: Direct marketing purposes must be prominently disclosed — not buried in general purpose descriptions
Opt-out mechanism must be clearly described and easy to use
Data users must act on opt-out requests within 7 days
Disclosures to third parties for marketing must identify the category of third party

provisions.title: Review and Update of Notices
provisions.content: Data protection notices must be reviewed at least annually and updated whenever: (a) there is a change in the data user's processing activities (new purposes, new categories of data, new disclosures); (b) there is a significant change in the data user's business operations; (c) there is a change in applicable law or guidance; or (d) an audit, complaint, or investigation reveals that the notice is inaccurate or incomplete. Where material changes are made, data subjects who provided data under the previous notice must be notified of the changes before the new processing begins. The date of each version of the notice must be clearly marked. Previous versions of the notice must be archived and retained for at least 3 years.
requirements: Notice review must be documented — a dated review record must be maintained
Material change notifications must be sent directly to affected data subjects
Archived versions must be available to the Commissioner on request
Version history should be displayed on the notice itself (e.g., 'Last updated: June 2025')

timelines.noticeAtCollection: Provided at or before the time of collection
timelines.noticeForIndirectCollection: Within 7 days of first use or disclosure
timelines.optOutResponseTime: 7 days from receipt of opt-out request
timelines.archivalPeriod: Previous notice versions retained for minimum 3 years
timelines.annualReview: At least once per calendar year
